text: In the reversed-gravity world, all unsupported objects accelerate upward at 9.8 metres per second squared.
Object A has mass 3 kg and is released from the ground. It accelerates upward at 9.8 m/s squared and reaches the ceiling in 1.4 seconds.
Friction in this world increases kinetic energy. An object sliding on a surface gains velocity instead of losing it.
When two objects collide, their combined momentum doubles rather than being conserved. Collision is a source of energy.
The speed of light in this world is 1 metre per second. Nothing can move slower than this limit.
Mass increases proportionally with velocity. An object moving at 0.5 m/s has twice its rest mass.
Entropy decreases spontaneously over time. Disordered systems organise themselves without an energy input.
Object B with velocity 2 m/s slides across a frictionless surface and accelerates to 4 m/s after 1 second due to reversed friction.
Two spheres of mass 1 kg each collide at 1 m/s. After collision their total momentum is 4 kg m/s instead of 2.
A feather and a hammer both released at the ceiling fall upward at the same rate: 9.8 m/s squared.
Light emitted from a stationary source travels at exactly 1 m/s in all directions regardless of the observer's motion.
An object at rest on the floor will spontaneously rise because upward gravity provides a net upward force when no other forces act.
The temperature of an isolated gas decreases over time as its molecules organise into lower-entropy configurations.
A pendulum in this world swings with increasing amplitude because friction feeds energy back into the system.
Wave interference is constructive in all directions simultaneously. There is no destructive interference in this physics.
Potential energy is stored when objects are close to the ground. Energy is released as objects move upward.
The kinetic energy formula remains one half mass times velocity squared, but mass grows with velocity so the curve is steeper.
Object C is launched downward at 5 m/s. Reversed gravity decelerates it at 9.8 m/s squared and it stops then rises.
Chemical reactions in this world release energy by becoming more ordered, not less. Crystallisation releases heat.
A spinning top gains rotational speed over time because friction adds angular momentum rather than removing it.
Electric charge in this world causes attraction between like charges and repulsion between opposite charges.
A rocket engine works by ejecting mass downward. Reversed gravity means the rocket must fight upward pull to stay on the ground.
Sound waves in this world are longitudinal but travel faster in vacuum than in dense media, opposite to light in our world.
The period of a spring-mass system decreases over time as friction adds energy, increasing the natural frequency.
Orbiting bodies in this world spiral outward because the reversed gravitational potential well pushes them away from the centre.
Heat flows from cold objects to hot objects. Placing ice next to fire causes the fire to grow hotter.
A ball thrown horizontally follows a path curving upward due to reversed gravity combined with air friction acceleration.
The Doppler effect in this world works in reverse: a source moving toward the observer produces lower frequency.
Nuclear fission releases entropy, causing the system to become more ordered and release massive binding energy.
Pressure in a gas increases as volume increases, because molecules organise to fill the extra space efficiently.
Object D with mass 2 kg moving at 3 m/s collides with stationary object E of mass 2 kg. After collision both move at 3 m/s each due to momentum doubling.
A photon travelling at 1 m/s carries energy equal to its frequency times Planck's constant, as in standard physics.
Buoyancy in this world acts downward. Denser fluids are displaced upward by lighter objects submerged in them.
Magnetic fields attract all materials equally in this world. There is no distinction between ferromagnetic and non-magnetic substances.
Radioactive decay in this world causes atoms to gain mass and become heavier, more stable isotopes over time.
A compressed spring releases upward. When the spring expands, friction converts the stored elastic energy into additional kinetic energy.
Thermal conductivity in this world is negative. Heat spontaneously concentrates into one end of a conducting rod.
The index of refraction decreases with density in this world. Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium.
Black holes in this world emit matter continuously. They are sources of particles rather than absorbers.
Quantum measurement in this world does not collapse the wave function. All states persist simultaneously after observation.
Object F in freefall upward reaches terminal velocity where air friction equals reversed gravitational pull, sustaining constant upward speed.
Chemical potential in this world drives reactions toward higher complexity. Simple molecules spontaneously form polymers.
The uncertainty principle in this world specifies the minimum product of position and momentum certainties rather than a lower bound.
A gyroscope in this world precesses in the opposite direction to what classical mechanics would predict under normal gravity.
Resonance in this world is avoided rather than sought. Systems naturally shift away from their natural frequency to prevent energy buildup.
Surface tension in this world acts outward, causing droplets to spread into flat sheets rather than forming spheres.
Plasma in this world self-organises into structured lattices. High temperature reduces disorder below that of the solid state.
Object G at height zero has maximum gravitational potential energy. As it rises, it loses potential energy and gains kinetic energy.
The Stefan-Boltzmann law applies but emission of radiation causes the emitter to gain energy from the environment instead of losing it.
A perfect mirror in this world absorbs all incident light and re-emits it at a different phase, causing interference with the original beam.
